content:
# Mark Chapter 15 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Mark Chapter 15 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 15:1 — "consultation" vs. "council" vs. "urgent meeting" for συμβούλιον ποιήσαντες

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having-made a-council" | Literal; συμβούλιον = council/consultation. |
| LOGOS | "held a consultation" | Formal; emphasizes the deliberative act. |
| PNEUMA | "called an urgent meeting" | Dynamic; adds "urgent" for narrative force. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "held a consultation" — συμβούλιον ποιήσαντες describes a formal deliberative action. PNEUMA's "urgent" is editorially inserted; the urgency is conveyed by "as soon as it was morning," not the noun itself.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:1 — "delivered him over" vs. "delivered him" vs. "handed him over" for παρέδωκαν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "delivered [him] to Pilate" | Literal; παραδίδωμι = hand over/deliver. |
| LOGOS | "delivered him over to Pilate" | Formal; adds "over" for emphasis. |
| PNEUMA | "handed him over to Pilate" | Dynamic; idiomatic English. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "delivered him over to Pilate" — "delivered over" captures the judicial transfer. This continues the established pattern of rendering παραδίδωμι in non-Judas contexts as "deliver" rather than "betray."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:3 — "accused him of many things" vs. "piled on accusation after accusation" for κατηγόρουν αὐτοῦ πολλά

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "were-accusing him of-many-[things]" | Literal; imperfect tense preserved. |
| LOGOS | "accused him of many things" | Formal; straightforward. |
| PNEUMA | "piled on accusation after accusation" | Dynamic; vivid repetition. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "accused him of many things" — PNEUMA's rendering is vivid but introduces a rhetorical figure (repetition) absent from the Greek. The imperfect tense (ongoing action) is noted but need not be dramatized.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:5 — "amazed" vs. "marveling" vs. "astonished" for θαυμάζειν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Pilate was-marveling" | Literal; θαυμάζω = marvel/wonder. |
| LOGOS | "Pilate was amazed" | Formal; idiomatic English. |
| PNEUMA | "which astonished Pilate" | Dynamic; restructured syntax. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Pilate was amazed" — θαυμάζω conveys surprise mixed with wonder. "Amazed" is the best single-word equivalent. "Marveling" (EMET) sounds overly positive; "astonished" (PNEUMA) is acceptable but the restructured syntax departs from Mark's paratactic style.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:10 — "envy" vs. "jealousy" for φθόνον

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "because-of envy" | Literal; φθόνος = envy. |
| LOGOS | "out of envy" | Formal; same word. |
| PNEUMA | "out of jealousy" | Dynamic; modern synonym. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "out of envy" — φθόνος specifically denotes envy (resentment at another's advantage), not jealousy (fear of losing what one has). The distinction matters: the chief priests envy Jesus's authority and popularity.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 15:15 — "scourged" vs. "flogged" for φραγελλώσας

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having-flogged Jesus" | Literal; φραγελλόω = flog with the flagellum. |
| LOGOS | "having scourged Jesus" | Formal; "scourged" carries judicial weight. |
| PNEUMA | "had Jesus flogged" | Dynamic; same base word. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "having scourged Jesus" — "scourged" is the traditional English rendering and carries the full weight of Roman judicial punishment. "Flogged" is accurate but slightly more generic. The Latin loanword φραγελλόω specifically refers to the Roman flagellum.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:16 — "palace/Praetorium" vs. "courtyard" vs. "governor's headquarters" for αὐλῆς, ὅ ἐστιν πραιτώριον

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "inside the courtyard — which is [the] Praetorium" | Literal; preserves both terms and the Latinism. |
| LOGOS | "inside the palace, that is, the governor's headquarters" | Formal; interprets both terms. |
| PNEUMA | "inside the courtyard of the governor's headquarters" | Dynamic; merges both terms. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "inside the palace, that is, the governor's headquarters" — Mark's own explanatory gloss (ὅ ἐστιν πραιτώριον) shows he is translating for his audience. LOGOS mirrors this by rendering αὐλή as "palace" (its extended sense) and πραιτώριον as "governor's headquarters." EMET's retention of "Praetorium" preserves the Latin loanword but defeats Mark's purpose in glossing it.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:16 — "battalion" vs. "cohort" vs. "regiment" for σπεῖραν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the whole cohort" | Literal; σπεῖρα = Roman cohort (~600 soldiers). |
| LOGOS | "the whole battalion" | Formal; modern military equivalent. |
| PNEUMA | "the entire regiment" | Dynamic; larger modern unit. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the whole battalion" — σπεῖρα technically denotes a Roman cohort (roughly 480-600 soldiers). "Battalion" is the closest modern equivalent. "Regiment" (PNEUMA) implies a larger unit than intended. "Cohort" (EMET) is technically precise but unfamiliar to most readers.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:17 — "purple cloak" vs. "purple" vs. "purple robe" for πορφύραν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they-dress him [in] purple" | Literal; no garment specified — just the color. |
| LOGOS | "clothed him in a purple cloak" | Formal; specifies the garment. |
| PNEUMA | "dressed him in a purple robe" | Dynamic; specifies as "robe." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "clothed him in a purple cloak" — the Greek πορφύρα refers to the purple fabric/garment. "Cloak" is specified because Mark later says they "stripped him of the purple" (v. 20), implying a removable outer garment. "Cloak" and "robe" are both defensible; "cloak" is preferred for consistency with the military mockery context (a soldier's cloak standing in for a royal robe).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:19 — "mock worship" vs. "were-worshiping" vs. "fake worship" for προσεκύνουν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "bending the knees, were-worshiping him" | Literal; preserves the verb without editorial comment. |
| LOGOS | "kneeling down in mock worship" | Formal; adds "mock" to clarify irony. |
| PNEUMA | "knelt before him in fake worship" | Dynamic; "fake" makes irony explicit. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "kneeling down in mock worship" — the soldiers' προσκύνησις is clearly ironic in context. "Mock worship" (LOGOS) makes this explicit without being colloquial. "Fake" (PNEUMA) is too informal. EMET's bare "were-worshiping" could confuse readers about the soldiers' intent, though Mark's irony is that they unwittingly do what is actually appropriate.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:22 — "Place of a Skull" vs. "Skull Hill" for Κρανίου Τόπος

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Place of-a-Skull" | Literal; Κρανίου Τόπος. |
| LOGOS | "Place of a Skull" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "Skull Hill" | Dynamic; interprets τόπος as "hill." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Place of a Skull" — τόπος means "place," not "hill." PNEUMA's "Skull Hill" imports the later tradition (and perhaps Calvary/calvaria associations) that the site was a hill. Mark says only "place."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 15:23 — "offered" vs. "were-giving" vs. "wine drugged with myrrh" for ἐδίδουν... ἐσμυρνισμένον οἶνον

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they-were-giving him wine mixed-with-myrrh" | Literal; "mixed with myrrh." |
| LOGOS | "They offered him wine mixed with myrrh" | Formal; "offered" for ἐδίδουν. |
| PNEUMA | "They offered him wine drugged with myrrh" | Dynamic; "drugged" interprets the purpose. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "offered him wine mixed with myrrh" — ἐσμυρνισμένον simply means "mixed/flavored with myrrh." PNEUMA's "drugged" imports an interpretation (that the myrrh was a sedative), which, while historically plausible, goes beyond the verb's semantic range.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:24 — "casting lots" vs. "gambling" for βάλλοντες κλῆρον

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "casting lots upon them" | Literal; βάλλω κλῆρον = cast lots. |
| LOGOS | "casting lots for them" | Formal; same idiom. |
| PNEUMA | "by gambling for them" | Dynamic; modernizes. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "casting lots for them" — βάλλοντες κλῆρον is a specific ancient practice (drawing lots) that may or may not resemble modern gambling. "Casting lots" preserves the biblical idiom and its Psalm 22:18 echo.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 15:25 — "the third hour" vs. "nine in the morning" for ὥρα τρίτη

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the third hour" | Literal; preserves ancient time-reckoning. |
| LOGOS | "the third hour" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "nine in the morning" | Dynamic; converts to modern time. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the third hour" — preserving the ancient time system maintains the structural parallel with "the sixth hour" (v. 33) and "the ninth hour" (v. 34), which create a three-stage temporal architecture for the crucifixion narrative. PNEUMA's conversion is helpful but disrupts this literary pattern.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 15:27 — "robbers" vs. "criminals" for λῃστάς

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "two robbers" | Literal; λῃστής = robber/brigand. |
| LOGOS | "two robbers" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "two criminals" | Dynamic; generalizes. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "two robbers" — λῃστής specifically means robber or brigand, not generic "criminal." The same word was used in 14:48 ("Have you come out as against a robber?"), and the verbal connection is important. This is also the word Josephus uses for insurrectionists/bandits.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 15:28 — "lawless" vs. "outlaws" for ἀνόμων

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "He was counted with the lawless" | Literal; ἄνομος = lawless/without law. |
| LOGOS | "He was counted with the lawless" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "He was counted among the outlaws" | Dynamic; "outlaws" is more concrete. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "He was counted with the lawless" — ἀνόμων means "lawless ones," and "the lawless" preserves the Isaiah 53:12 allusion in its traditional form. "Outlaws" (PNEUMA) shifts the register toward the colloquial.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 15:29 — "derided" vs. "were-blaspheming" vs. "hurled insults" for ἐβλασφήμουν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "were-blaspheming him" | Literal; βλασφημέω = blaspheme/revile. |
| LOGOS | "derided him" | Formal; renders the social act. |
| PNEUMA | "hurled insults at him" | Dynamic; vivid action. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "derided him" — βλασφημέω in this context means to revile or speak abusively (not "blaspheme" in the theological sense, which would be ironic given that those mocking Jesus are the ones actually blaspheming). "Derided" captures the contemptuous mockery. EMET's "blaspheming" is technically literal but potentially confusing. PNEUMA's "hurled insults" is vivid but lacks the specific tone of contempt.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:29 — "Aha!" vs. "Ha!" for Οὐά

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Ha!" | Literal; Οὐά = exclamation of derision. |
| LOGOS | "Aha!" | Formal; slightly more mocking. |
| PNEUMA | "Ha! So you were going to..." | Dynamic; "Ha" plus expanded syntax. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Aha!" — Οὐά is an exclamation of contemptuous mockery. "Aha!" captures the jeering tone slightly better than "Ha!" which can express surprise or laughter. A minor divergence.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:32 — "the Christ, the King of Israel" vs. "this 'Messiah,' this 'King of Israel'" for ὁ Χριστὸς ὁ βασιλεὺς Ἰσραήλ

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Let the Christ, the King of Israel" | Literal; preserves titles. |
| LOGOS | "Let the Christ, the King of Israel" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "Let this 'Messiah,' this 'King of Israel'" | Dynamic; scare quotes signal mockery. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Let the Christ, the King of Israel" — the mockery is clear from context. PNEUMA's scare quotes editorialize; Mark lets the irony speak for itself. Consistent with the established rendering of Χριστός as "Christ" throughout.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 15:33 — "darkness over the whole land" vs. time notation (ancient vs. modern)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "when the sixth hour came, darkness came upon the whole land until the ninth hour" | Literal; ancient hours preserved. |
| LOGOS | "When the sixth hour came, there was darkness over the whole land until the ninth hour" | Formal; same structure. |
| PNEUMA | "At noon, darkness fell over the entire land and lasted until three in the afternoon" | Dynamic; converts to modern time. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "When the sixth hour came, there was darkness over the whole land until the ninth hour" — consistent with the decision at v. 25 to preserve ancient time-reckoning. The three hours (third, sixth, ninth) form a deliberate liturgical structure in Mark's Passion narrative.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:34 — "forsaken" vs. "abandoned" for ἐγκατέλιπες

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "why have-you-forsaken me?" | Literal; ἐγκαταλείπω = forsake/abandon. |
| LOGOS | "why have you forsaken me?" | Formal; same. |
| PNEUMA | "why have you abandoned me?" | Dynamic; modern synonym. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "why have you forsaken me?" — "forsaken" is the traditional rendering of Psalm 22:1 and carries a gravitas that "abandoned" does not. "Abandoned" is accurate but slightly clinical. The archaic weight of "forsaken" matches the gravity of the cry of dereliction.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 15:37 — "breathed his last" vs. "expired" vs. "died" for ἐξέπνευσεν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "expired" | Literal; ἐκπνέω = breathe out/expire. |
| LOGOS | "breathed his last" | Formal; English idiom. |
| PNEUMA | "died" | Dynamic; simplest term. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "breathed his last" — ἐκπνέω literally means "to breathe out." "Breathed his last" preserves the physicality of the Greek (the final exhalation) while being idiomatically natural. "Expired" (EMET) is etymologically accurate but clinical in modern English. "Died" (PNEUMA) is too blunt for this climactic moment and loses the breath imagery that connects to πνεῦμα (spirit/breath).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:39 — "the Son of God" vs. "God's Son" for Θεοῦ Υἱός

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Truly this man was God's Son" | Literal; preserves the anarthrous genitive-first Greek word order. |
| LOGOS | "Truly this man was the Son of God" | Formal; supplies the article for English idiom. |
| PNEUMA | "This man really was the Son of God" | Dynamic; "really" for ἀληθῶς. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Truly this man was the Son of God" — the Greek is anarthrous (ΘΕΟΥ ΥΙΟΣ, without the definite article), which EMET rightly notes. However, Mark intends this as the climactic christological confession forming an inclusio with 1:1. "The Son of God" is the theologically appropriate English rendering. "Truly" is preferred over "really" (PNEUMA) for register. EMET's "God's Son" is a valid alternative that preserves the Greek ambiguity, but the consensus follows the tradition of reading this as Mark's definitive confession.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:39 — "stood facing him" vs. "had-stood opposite him" vs. "standing guard in front of" for παρεστηκὼς ἐξ ἐναντίας αὐτοῦ

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the centurion who had-stood opposite him" | Literal; perfect participle + prepositional phrase. |
| LOGOS | "the centurion, who stood facing him" | Formal; "facing him" for ἐξ ἐναντίας. |
| PNEUMA | "The Roman centurion standing guard in front of Jesus" | Dynamic; adds "Roman" and "guard" for context. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the centurion, who stood facing him" — PNEUMA adds "Roman" (implicit but not in the text) and "standing guard" (interpretive). The Greek says only that the centurion "stood opposite/facing him." The face-to-face position is theologically significant: the centurion sees Jesus die and responds with the confession. No editorial additions needed.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:43 — "looking for" vs. "awaiting" vs. "waiting for" for προσδεχόμενος

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "was-awaiting the kingdom of God" | Literal; προσδέχομαι = await/expect. |
| LOGOS | "was himself looking for the kingdom of God" | Formal; "looking for" suggests active expectation. |
| PNEUMA | "was himself waiting for God's kingdom" | Dynamic; "waiting for" is natural English. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "was himself looking for the kingdom of God" — προσδέχομαι carries the sense of expectant waiting. "Looking for" combines active anticipation with hope. "Awaiting" (EMET) is slightly stiff; "waiting for" (PNEUMA) is more passive than the Greek implies.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:44 — "was surprised" vs. "was-surprised" vs. different construction for ἐθαύμασεν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Pilate was-surprised that he-had already died" | Literal; θαυμάζω here = be surprised. |
| LOGOS | "Pilate was surprised to hear that he was already dead" | Formal; adds "to hear" for smooth English. |
| PNEUMA | "Pilate was surprised that Jesus was already dead" | Dynamic; names Jesus for clarity. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Pilate was surprised to hear that he was already dead" — all three agree on the core meaning. LOGOS's addition of "to hear" is a minor smoothing that reflects the implied context (Joseph told Pilate). Naming "Jesus" (PNEUMA) is unnecessary since the referent is clear.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:45 — "body" vs. "corpse" for πτῶμα

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he-granted the corpse to Joseph" | Literal; πτῶμα = corpse/fallen body. |
| LOGOS | "he granted the corpse to Joseph" | Formal; same rendering. |
| PNEUMA | "Pilate released the body to Joseph" | Dynamic; uses "body" throughout. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "he granted the body to Joseph" in the consensus text for readability, while noting in the council notes that Mark deliberately shifts from σῶμα (v. 43, "body" — Joseph's request) to πτῶμα (v. 45, "corpse" — Pilate's grant). EMET and LOGOS correctly render πτῶμα as "corpse," and the shift is theologically stark. However, "corpse" in the consensus text would be jarring. The shift is documented here for the record.

**Status:** Resolved as compromise — "body" in text, Greek shift noted.

---

## 15:46 — "linen shroud" vs. "linen-cloth" vs. "linen sheet" for σινδόνα

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a-linen-cloth" / "the linen-cloth" | Literal; σινδών = linen cloth. |
| LOGOS | "a linen shroud" / "the linen shroud" | Formal; "shroud" specifies burial use. |
| PNEUMA | "a linen sheet" / "the linen" | Dynamic; "sheet" specifies form. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "a linen shroud" — σινδών is the same word used in 14:51-52 for the young man's garment (where "linen cloth" was adopted). Here, in the burial context, "shroud" is the appropriate English specification. The verbal echo with 14:51 is maintained through the "linen" root; the shift from "cloth" to "shroud" reflects the shift from a garment worn to a garment wrapping the dead.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| **Time notation** (ancient hours vs. modern conversion) | vv. 25, 33, 34 | Ancient hours preserved for liturgical structure |
| **Register/formality of violence vocabulary** (scourged vs. flogged; derided vs. hurled insults) | vv. 15, 29 | Formal register preferred for gravity of the Passion |
| **PNEUMA's editorial additions** (urgent, drugged, Roman, gambling, scare quotes) | vv. 1, 23, 24, 32, 39 | Not adopted; Mark's restraint preserved |
| **Christological title** (the Son of God vs. God's Son) | v. 39 | "The Son of God" — inclusio with 1:1 |
| **Death vocabulary** (breathed his last vs. expired vs. died) | v. 37 | "Breathed his last" — preserves breath imagery |
| **σῶμα/πτῶμα distinction** (body vs. corpse) | vv. 43, 45 | "Body" in consensus text; Greek shift documented |
| **Topographical/military terms** (Golgotha, cohort, Praetorium) | vv. 16, 22 | Translated for clarity; Latin/Aramaic terms glossed |

**Overall Pattern Analysis:** Mark 15 is the crucifixion narrative — the theological climax of the Gospel. The translators show strong agreement on the core narrative: the trial before Pilate, the mocking, the crucifixion itself, and the burial. The most significant disagreements concern (1) the centurion's confession in v. 39, where the anarthrous Greek creates ambiguity about "the Son of God" vs. "God's Son" vs. "a son of God"; (2) the time notation system, where PNEUMA consistently modernizes while the consensus preserves Mark's three-stage temporal structure (third hour, sixth hour, ninth hour); (3) the cry of dereliction in v. 34, where "forsaken" vs. "abandoned" reflects a choice between traditional weight and modern clarity; and (4) the σῶμα/πτῶμα shift in vv. 43-45, where Mark's deliberate vocabulary change is theologically significant but difficult to render naturally. PNEUMA's tendency toward editorial addition (adding "urgent," "drugged," "Roman," scare quotes) is most pronounced in this chapter, and the consensus consistently prefers Mark's own restraint — letting the narrative events carry their own devastating weight.
